title: Radioisotope Sources and Exposure Devices
narrationScript: In this lesson we get familiar with the gamma side of radiography, the sealed radioactive sources we use and the exposure devices that carry them. A radioisotope does not need power or a warm up. It simply emits radiation all the time, every second of every day, which is exactly why the equipment around it has to be built to keep that radiation locked away until we deliberately let it out. Start with the source itself. The active material is a small pellet or a short stack of tiny discs of an unstable isotope. That active material is sealed inside a small welded capsule, usually two layers of stainless steel, one capsule welded shut inside another. This double wall is what makes it a sealed source. The whole point is that the radioactive material can never leak, rub off, or become airborne, even if the capsule is dropped or scraped. The sealed capsule is fastened to a short flexible cable, and together the capsule and cable are called the source assembly or the pigtail. We never touch the capsule. We handle only the far end of the cable. Now the isotopes. The workhorse of field radiography is iridium one ninety two. Its half life is about seventy four days, its average energy is about zero point three five million electron volts, and it suits steel from roughly half an inch up to about two and a half inches. Because seventy four days is short, an iridium source loses strength quickly and has to be replaced a few times a year. Cobalt sixty is the high energy gamma source. It has a long half life of about five point two seven years and two strong energies near one point one seven and one point three three million electron volts, so it penetrates thick steel, roughly one and a half to eight inches, but it needs heavy shielding and large standoff distances. Selenium seventy five sits at the low end. Its half life is about one hundred twenty days and its energy is lower, which gives high contrast on thin wall steel and small parts. There are two more source types you should recognize even if you rarely use them. A beta source emits electrons rather than gamma rays. On its own a beta particle barely penetrates, but when those electrons slam into a heavy metal target they produce X rays through a process called bremsstrahlung, which means braking radiation, and that is how a beta source can be turned into a usable X ray emitter. Neutron sources are different again. They emit neutrons, which are uncharged, and they are used for neutron radiography, a specialized method that images low density materials like plastics, adhesives, and explosives hidden inside metal, things a normal gamma image cannot show. A common neutron source pairs an alpha emitter with beryllium, or uses californium two fifty two, which fissions on its own. Now the exposure device, often just called the camera. Inside is a block of depleted uranium or sometimes tungsten that shields the source in every direction while it is stored. The source assembly sits in an S shaped or curved tube through that shield, so there is no straight line for radiation to escape. To make an exposure we connect a drive cable and a guide tube, crank the source out of the shield, down the guide tube, and into the collimator at the work. After the exposure we crank it all the way back and confirm it is home. The most important rule in gamma work follows directly from how the device works. The source is always live. You never trust the crank, the indicator, or your memory. You survey with a calibrated meter after every single exposure and as you approach the camera, because a source that fails to fully retract is the classic way radiographers get seriously overexposed. Treat every device as hot until your meter tells you otherwise.
imagePrompt: A radiographer kneeling beside a yellow gamma-ray exposure device on a pipeline right-of-way, attaching a crank-out drive cable while holding a survey meter, with a guide tube running to a collimator at a welded pipe joint in low evening light.

sections.heading: Radioisotope Sources: Always On
sections.body:
Gamma radiography uses a **radioisotope** - an unstable form of an element that emits penetrating radiation as its nucleus decays. Unlike an X-ray tube, a radioisotope needs no electricity and no warm-up. It emits radiation continuously, around the clock, and there is no off switch. That single fact shapes everything about the equipment: the source must be permanently shielded and only deliberately exposed.

The radiation comes from **radioactive decay**, in which the nucleus sheds energy to become more stable. The rate of decay is fixed for each isotope and is described by its **half-life** - the time for half the atoms to decay - which we use to predict how source strength falls over time. Field radiography relies almost entirely on **gamma-ray** emitters, but you should also recognize **beta/bremsstrahlung** sources and **neutron** sources, because the CP-105 outline covers all three and each images things the others cannot. The remaining sections build from the sealed capsule outward to the exposure device that carries it.

sections.heading: Sealed-Source Design and Fabrication
sections.body:
An industrial source is a **sealed source**: the active material is enclosed so it cannot escape under normal use, wear, or a credible accident. Fabrication starts with the active material formed as a small pellet or a short stack of thin metal discs - for example, iridium foil discs stacked to the needed activity. That material is placed in an inner **capsule**, typically welded **stainless steel**, which is then sealed inside an **outer capsule** and welded shut. The result is a **doubly encapsulated** source: two independent welded walls between the radioactive material and the environment.

This construction does two jobs. It contains the material so nothing can rub off, leak, or become airborne, and it gives the source mechanical ruggedness for field handling. Each sealed source carries a manufacturer's serial number and a leak-test history; periodic **wipe (leak) tests** confirm the welds remain intact. The capsule is fastened to a short flexible cable; the capsule plus cable is the **source assembly**, commonly called the **pigtail**. Technicians handle only the cable end - never the capsule. The size of the active pellet matters beyond safety: a physically small, high-**specific-activity** source acts more like a point and produces sharper radiographs.

sections.caption: Cutaway of a doubly encapsulated sealed source assembly: active pellet, inner and outer welded stainless capsules, and the connector that joins it to the drive cable.

sections.heading: Gamma-Ray Sources: Ir-192, Co-60, Se-75
sections.body:
Three gamma emitters cover almost all industrial work, chosen by thickness and the contrast you need.

- **Iridium-192** is the workhorse. Half-life about **74 days**, average energy about **0.35 MeV**, well suited to steel from roughly **0.5 to 2.5 in.** Its short half-life means rapid decay and replacement several times a year.
- **Cobalt-60** is the high-energy choice. Half-life about **5.27 years**, with two strong gamma energies near **1.17 and 1.33 MeV**, penetrating steel from roughly **1.5 to 8 in.** It demands heavy shielding and large exclusion distances.
- **Selenium-75** serves thin-wall, high-contrast work. Half-life about **120 days** and lower energy, giving better contrast on thin steel and small-diameter parts.

A useful output rule of thumb: Ir-192 produces about **0.5 R/h per curie at 1 m**, and Co-60 about **1.3 R/h per curie at 1 m** (approximate, depending on source construction). Energy sets penetrating power; activity (in curies) sets how fast you get an exposure.

sections.caption: Common industrial gamma sources and where each is used.

sections.heading: Beta/Bremsstrahlung and Neutron Sources
sections.body:
Two specialized source types round out the picture.

A **beta source** emits high-speed electrons (beta particles). Beta particles alone penetrate very little, so a beta emitter is rarely used to shoot through metal directly. Instead, when those electrons strike a high-atomic-number **target**, they decelerate sharply and convert part of their energy into X-rays - a process called **bremsstrahlung** (German for *braking radiation*). This is the same continuous-spectrum mechanism that produces X-rays in a tube, and it lets a beta emitter act as a compact X-ray source.

**Neutron sources** emit **neutrons**, which carry no charge and interact with matter very differently from gamma rays. Neutron radiography excels at imaging **low-density** materials - plastics, adhesives, lubricants, explosives, and organic residues - even when they sit inside or behind metal, the opposite of gamma's strength. Industrial neutron sources include an **alpha-emitter mixed with beryllium** (for example americium-beryllium), which produces neutrons through an alpha-neutron reaction, or **californium-252**, which emits neutrons by spontaneous fission. Reactor and accelerator beams are used in fixed facilities.

sections.title: Pick the source by thickness and contrast
sections.body: Match **energy** to thickness and **activity** to throughput. Use **Se-75** for thin, high-contrast work, **Ir-192** for the broad mid-range of steel, and **Co-60** only when the section is too thick for iridium. Higher energy penetrates more but lowers contrast, so do not reach for cobalt when iridium will do.

sections.heading: Exposure Device Characteristics
sections.body:
The **exposure device** - field name **camera** or **projector** - stores the source safely and lets you project it to the work on demand. Its core is a heavy shield, usually **depleted uranium** (very dense, so the device stays compact) or sometimes tungsten or lead, surrounding an **S-shaped channel**. The S-tube means there is no straight-line path for radiation to leak out while the source is parked in the center. A locking mechanism secures the source in the shielded position, and the device is rated by category (for example, a portable Category II device for field use).

To make an exposure you attach a **drive (control) cable** at one port and a **guide tube** with a **collimator** at the other, then crank the source out of the shield, through the guide tube, and into position at the part. After exposing, you crank it fully back and lock it. Because the source is **always emitting**, the discipline is non-negotiable: **survey with a calibrated meter after every exposure and as you approach the device.** A source that fails to fully retract into the shield is the classic cause of serious radiographer overexposure - never trust the crank stop or the indicator alone, only the meter.

sections.caption: Cross-section of a gamma exposure device showing the depleted-uranium shield, S-shaped source channel, drive-cable and guide-tube ports, and the source projected to the collimator.

interactive.title: Find the parts of the exposure device
interactive.intro: This is the cross-section of a gamma-ray exposure device with the source parked in the shielded position. Click the feature described in each prompt.

regions.label: Depleted-uranium shield
regions.feedback: Correct. The dense depleted-uranium shield surrounds the source on all sides while it is stored, keeping external exposure low.

regions.label: S-shaped source channel
regions.feedback: That is the S-tube the source rides in. Its curve prevents a straight-line radiation leak, but the prompt asked for the shield material around it.

regions.label: Drive-cable port
regions.feedback: That is where the drive (control) cable connects to crank the source out - not the shield.

regions.label: Collimator at the work
regions.feedback: That is the collimator where the source is projected for the exposure, downstream of the shield.

checkQuestions.question: Why is an industrial gamma source built as a doubly encapsulated, welded stainless capsule?
options: To increase the gamma energy it emits
To contain the radioactive material so it cannot leak or become airborne, even if damaged
To shorten the half-life for faster decay
To allow the technician to handle the capsule directly
checkQuestions.explanation: Two independent welded walls keep the active material sealed in under normal use and credible accidents; encapsulation does not change energy, half-life, or make the capsule safe to touch.

checkQuestions.question: A job requires radiography of 6 in. thick steel. Which gamma source is the appropriate choice?
options: Selenium-75, because it gives high contrast
Iridium-192, because it is the workhorse source
Cobalt-60, because its higher energy penetrates thick sections
Any of them, since energy does not affect penetration
checkQuestions.explanation: Co-60's 1.17 and 1.33 MeV energies penetrate steel roughly 1.5-8 in.; Ir-192 tops out near 2.5 in. and Se-75 is for thin wall.

checkQuestions.question: An Ir-192 source has a half-life of about 74 days. Compared with Co-60 (about 5.27 years), the practical consequence is that the iridium source:
options: Lasts longer and is replaced less often
Decays faster and must be replaced several times a year
Emits higher-energy gamma rays
Requires heavier shielding than cobalt
checkQuestions.explanation: A short half-life means activity falls quickly, so iridium sources are exchanged a few times per year; cobalt's long half-life and higher energy are separate properties.

checkQuestions.question: A beta source by itself penetrates metal poorly. How is it used to produce X-rays for radiography?
options: By raising its temperature until it glows
By directing its electrons onto a high-atomic-number target to produce bremsstrahlung
By mixing it with beryllium to make neutrons
By doubling its activity
checkQuestions.explanation: When the beta particles (electrons) strike a heavy-metal target and decelerate, they emit bremsstrahlung (braking) X-rays - the same continuous-spectrum process used in an X-ray tube.

checkQuestions.question: After completing a gamma exposure, what is the single most important action before approaching the camera?
options: Trust the crank stop and indicator that the source returned
Survey with a calibrated meter to confirm the source is fully shielded
Disconnect the guide tube immediately
Record the exposure time and move on
checkQuestions.explanation: Because the source is always emitting, a failure to fully retract is the classic overexposure cause; you confirm with a calibrated survey meter after every exposure and on approach, never the crank or indicator alone.

references: CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Basic RT Physics 6.1 (radioisotope sources)
CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Basic RT Physics 6.1.1-6.1.4 (sealed-source, gamma, beta/bremsstrahlung, neutron)
CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Basic RT Physics 6.2 (exposure device characteristics)
Regulatory practice for sealed sources and devices: 10 CFR 34-style requirements (typical NRC/Agreement-State)
